title: RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation at Scale
description: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is the dominant architecture for grounding LLMs in external knowledge. At Staff level, you must design RAG pipelines that handle 10M+ queries/day, optimize retrieval quality, and manage chunking/embedding/reranking at scale.

sections.title: RAG Architecture Overview
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: RAG decomposes knowledge-grounded generation into three stages. Indexing (offline): documents → chunks → embeddings → vector store. Retrieval (online): query → embed → nearest-neighbor search → top-k passages. Generation: retrieved passages + query → LLM → grounded answer. The key insight: RAG inverts the traditional approach — instead of cramming all knowledge into model weights (which is expensive and hard to update), keep the model lightweight and store knowledge externally where it can be updated independently.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: End-to-end latency budget for a RAG pipeline: retrieval (embed query ~10ms + vector search ~5-50ms + optional reranking ~5-100ms) = 50-200ms total retrieval. Generation: first-token latency depends on context length (e.g., 2K context → ~100ms prefill for 7B) + decode at ~20-40 tokens/s. Total p95: 300-800ms depending on output length and model size. 2K token context with RAG vs 128K full-context: RAG is 10-20× cheaper in KV cache memory and 2-5× faster in prefill time.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Corpus ingestion: chunk documents (256-512 tokens), embed with a single model, store in vector DB with metadata. 2) Query processing: embed query with the same model, retrieve top-20 passages. 3) Reranking: cross-encoder reranks top-20 down to top-3 for final context. 4) Generation: format context + query into prompt, call LLM. 5) Caching: cache frequent query embeddings (80%+ hit rate cuts retrieval cost by 5×).

search Interview Angle: "Why not just put all documents into the LLM's context window?" For a corpus of 10M documents, context window would need to be billions of tokens. Even with 2M context (Gemini), you'd need 5 passes to cover 10M docs. RAG's retrieval step narrows billions of tokens to the 2K most relevant — the search index is essentially a learned attention mechanism over the entire corpus, but computed efficiently with approximate nearest neighbor search instead of quadratic self-attention.
sections.codeBlock:
# RAG pipeline — three stages
import numpy as np

class RAGPipeline:
    def __init__(self, embed_model, vector_store, llm, reranker=None):
        self.embed = embed_model       # dim: 768 | 1024 | 1536 | 4096
        self.store = vector_store      # HNSW index (M=16, ef=40)
        self.llm = llm                 # Generator model
        self.reranker = reranker       # Optional cross-encoder

    def index(self, documents: list[str]):
        """Indexing stage: chunk → embed → store."""
        chunks = [doc[i:i+512] for doc in documents for i in range(0, len(doc), 384)]
        embeddings = self.embed(chunks)
        self.store.add(embeddings, chunks)

    def answer(self, query: str, top_k: int = 3):
        """Retrieval + generation."""
        q_emb = self.embed([query])[0]
        candidates = self.store.search(q_emb, k=20)  # First pass: ANN
        if self.reranker:
            candidates = self.reranker.rerank(query, candidates, k=top_k)
        context = "\n---\n".join(candidates)
        prompt = f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {query}\nAnswer:"
        return self.llm.generate(prompt)

# ponytail: 10M queries/day × 200ms = 2M seconds/day ≈ 23 GPU-days
# Staff target: bring retrieval p95 under 100ms via caching + HNSW tuning

sections.title: Chunking Strategies
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Chunking is the first and most impactful retrieval quality lever. If you chunk poorly, the best embedding model and vector DB can't save you. Three dominant strategies: Fixed-size (sliding window of N tokens with overlap), Semantic (split at sentence/document boundaries using embeddings or NLP heuristics), Recursive (parent chunks with overlapping child chunks — retrieve child, return parent for more context).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Fixed-size at 256 tokens: best for precise retrieval (high precision) — the chunk is focused on one topic. 512 tokens: best for recall-heavy tasks (the chunk has enough context to answer). 128 tokens: too small, loses context (retrieval recall drops ~15% vs 256). Optimal overlap: 10-20% of chunk size. A study across 10 RAG benchmarks found the optimal chunk size depends on the corpus: legal/academic (dense, long-form) → 512 tokens; conversational/support → 256 tokens; code → 64-128 tokens.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Benchmark 3 chunk sizes (128, 256, 512) on a golden eval set with queries and ground-truth passages. 2) Measure hit rate @1 and @5 — pick the size that maximizes hit rate @5. 3) Add 15% overlap (mitigates boundary artifacts where a query's answer straddles two chunks). 4) For complex documents (scientific papers, legal contracts), use recursive chunking: child chunks at 256 tokens for retrieval precision, parent chunks at 1K tokens for generation context. 5) Re-evaluate after 3 months — corpus distribution shifts can change optimal chunk size.

search Interview Angle: "A user asks 'What was the 2019 Q3 revenue?' but the exact figure is split across two chunks. What do you do?" Two answers: 1) Overlap: with 15-20% overlap, the boundary likely falls in the overlap region. 2) Recursive chunking: retrieve the child chunk that contains the partial number, then return the parent chunk (the full section) as context — the LLM gets the whole paragraph and can join the information. 3) Sentence-aware splitting: never split mid-sentence; use spaCy or NLTK sentence boundaries combined with the token budget.
sections.codeBlock:
# Chunking strategies with benchmarks
import re

def fixed_size_chunk(text: str, chunk_size: int = 512, overlap: int = 64) -> list[str]:
    """Fixed-size sliding window chunking."""
    tokens = text.split()  # Naive tokenization; use real tokenizer in prod
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(tokens), chunk_size - overlap):
        chunk = " ".join(tokens[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks

def semantic_chunk(text: str, max_tokens: int = 512) -> list[str]:
    """Sentence-aware chunking — never split mid-sentence."""
    sentences = re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', text)
    chunks, current = [], []
    current_len = 0
    for sent in sentences:
        sent_len = len(sent.split())
        if current_len + sent_len > max_tokens and current:
            chunks.append(" ".join(current))
            current, current_len = [], 0
        current.append(sent)
        current_len += sent_len
    if current:
        chunks.append(" ".join(current))
    return chunks

def benchmark_chunk_sizes(documents, queries, ground_truth, embed_model):
    """Find optimal chunk size for a corpus."""
    for size in [128, 256, 512, 1024]:
        chunks = []
        for doc in documents:
            chunks.extend(fixed_size_chunk(doc, chunk_size=size))
        embeds = embed_model(chunks)
        hits = sum(1 for q, gt in zip(queries, ground_truth)
                   if any(gt in c for c in chunks))
        print(f"Chunk {size:4d}: {len(chunks)} chunks, hit_rate={hits/len(queries):.0%}")

# ponytail: semantic chunking adds ~2ms per doc; fixed-size is faster.
# For ≤10M docs, semantic chunking is worth the extra cost.

sections.title: Embedding Models & Vector Databases
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Embedding models map text to dense vectors where semantic similarity ≈ cosine similarity. The choice of embedding model determines the ceiling on retrieval quality. The vector database determines the floor — how fast and accurately you can search those vectors at scale. At Staff level, you need to understand both: the embedding's dimensionality (tradeoff: higher dim = more expressiveness × more memory/compute) and the index algorithm's recall/speed tradeoff.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Major embedding models: OpenAI ada-002 (1536d, $0.13/M tokens), Cohere Embed v3 (1024d multilingual), BGE-large-en-v1.5 (1024d, open-source), E5-mistral-7b (4096d, best quality but expensive). MTEB leaderboard scores: E5-mistral-7b 66.6, BGE-large 64.2, ada-002 61.0. Vector DB comparison: Pinecone (managed, $0.10/GB/hr, 150ms p99 at 10M vectors), Weaviate (self-hosted, 50ms at 10M), Qdrant (self-hosted, 35ms at 10M with HNSW), Milvus (distributed, 20ms at 100M+). For <10M vectors, Qdrant or Weaviate on a single machine is sufficient; above 100M, Milvus with sharding.

track_changes HNSW Parameters: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is the de-facto ANN index. Three knobs: M (connections per node, default 16): higher M = better recall × more memory. efConstruction (search breadth during indexing, default 200): higher = better index quality × slower build. ef_search (search breadth during query, default 40): higher = better recall × slower query. M=16, efConstruction=200, ef_search=40 gives ~95% recall at 10ms for 1M vectors. Doubling M to 32 gives 97% recall at 15ms.

search Interview Angle: "You have 50M vectors with 1536 dimensions and need p95 latency < 50ms. Design the vector search system." Answer: HNSW index with M=24, efConstruction=300, ef_search=60. Memory: 50M × 1536 × 4 bytes (FP32) = 307GB for vectors + ~100GB for HNSW graph (M=24 → ~48 bytes per edge × 24 edges × 50M ≈ 57GB) = ~400GB. Shard across 4 machines (100GB each). Use IVF+PQ product quantization to reduce memory to 50GB (4× compression) with <5% recall loss. Partition by document source for filtered search.
sections.codeBlock:
# HNSW index parameters — recall vs speed tuning
import numpy as np

def hnsw_tradeoffs(M: int, ef_construction: int, n_vectors: int, dim: int):
    """Estimate HNSW memory and search cost."""
    # Memory: vectors + graph edges
    vector_bytes = n_vectors * dim * 4  # FP32
    graph_edges = n_vectors * M * 24    # Each edge ~24 bytes (neighbor ID + distance)
    total_mb = (vector_bytes + graph_edges) / 1e6

    # Search cost approximation: O(log N) for HNSW
    recall_est = min(1.0, 0.70 + 0.015 * M + 0.001 * ef_construction + 0.005 * 40)
    latency_ms = 1 + 0.15 * M + 0.02 * 40  # Rough model

    print(f"M={M:2d}, ef_con={ef_construction:3d}: "
          f"{total_mb:.0f}MB, recall~{recall_est:.0%}, latency~{latency_ms:.0f}ms")

print("HNSW parameter sweep for 10M vectors @ 1536d:")
for M in [8, 16, 24, 32]:
    hnsw_tradeoffs(M, 200, 10_000_000, 1536)

# Staff recommendation:
# M=16 for <10M vectors (balanced); M=24 for 10-100M; M=32 only if recall > 98% needed
# ef_search: start at 40, increase by 20 until recall plateaus
# ponytail: these are heuristics; benchmark on your actual data distribution

sections.title: Hybrid Search & Reranking
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Dense retrieval (embedding similarity) excels at semantic matching but misses exact keyword matches. Sparse retrieval (BM25) excels at exact term matching but misses paraphrases. Hybrid search combines both with reciprocal rank fusion (RRF): convert each result's rank to a score (1/(k + rank)), sum scores across both retrievers, and re-rank. Reranking with a cross-encoder (which attends to both query and document simultaneously) further refines the top-k — it's more accurate than bi-encoder retrieval but too slow to run on all candidates.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Hybrid search (BM25 + dense) improves recall @20 by 5-15% over dense-only across standard IR benchmarks (BEIR, MTEB). Reciprocal Rank Fusion with k=60 (typical constant): a document ranked 1st gets score 1/61 ≈ 0.0164, ranked 10th gets 1/70 ≈ 0.0143. Cross-encoder reranker (monoBERT, Cohere Rerank 3) improves top-5 accuracy by 10-20% over dense retrieval alone. Cohere Rerank 3: 2ms per query-doc pair on A100. Reranking top-100 costs 200ms; reranking top-20 costs 40ms — always rerank only the top-20 to 50 candidates.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Retrieve top-100 from dense (embedding similarity) + top-100 from sparse (BM25). 2) Fuse with RRF (k=60) to get top-50. 3) Run cross-encoder reranker on the fused top-50. 4) Take top-3 to 5 for LLM context. 5) Tune RRF constant k: lower k gives higher weight to top-ranked results (precision-focused), higher k flattens the distribution (recall-focused). Default k=60 is a safe starting point.

search Staff Engineer Question: "Your team reports dense-only retrieval misses long-tail queries with specific product codes. How do you fix it?" Answer: Add BM25 sparse retrieval and hybrid fusion. Product codes (e.g., 'XG-2000-β') are exact terms that embedding models handle poorly (they lack training examples for arbitrary alphanumeric sequences). BM25's term-frequency scoring captures these exactly. Combined with RRF, the hybrid approach gets the best of both: semantic matching for natural-language queries + exact matching for codes and entities. Monitor reranking latency: if it exceeds p95 budget, prune reranking candidates from 50 → 20.

sections.title: RAG Evaluation
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: RAG has two quality surfaces: retrieval quality (did we find the right passages?) and generation quality (did the LLM use those passages correctly?). They must be evaluated independently and jointly. A common anti-pattern: only measuring end-to-end answer accuracy. If the answer is wrong, you need to know whether retrieval or generation caused it.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Retrieval metrics: Hit Rate @k (ground-truth passage in top-k) — target HR@5 > 90%. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) — target > 0.80. NDCG@10 (discounted cumulative gain with graded relevance) — target > 0.85. Generation metrics: Faithfulness (does the answer contradict the retrieved context?) — RAGAS faithfulness score > 80%. Answer Relevance (does the answer address the query?) — > 85%. Context Precision (is the retrieved context free of irrelevant info?) — > 70%. A/B test RAG vs long-context: for factual QA, RAG typically wins by 5-10%; for open-ended summarization, long-context wins by 3-5%.

track_changes RAGAS Framework: RAGAS (RAG Assessment) provides automated evaluation components: 1) Faithfulness: decompose answer into atomic claims, check each against retrieved context. 2) Answer Relevance: generate questions from the answer, measure cosine similarity with original query. 3) Context Precision: rank retrieved passages by relevance, measure how well relevant passages rank above irrelevant. 4) Context Recall: what fraction of ground-truth claims appear in the retrieved context. Run RAGAS on 500 eval queries per deployment for a statistically significant score.

search Interview Angle: "Your RAG system achieves 85% faithfulness but 60% answer relevance. The LLM team says it's a retrieval problem, the retrieval team says it's a generation problem. How do you root-cause it?" Answer: Freeze one side and vary the other. 1) Oracle retrieval test: feed the ground-truth passage directly as context (skip retrieval). If answer relevance jumps to 90%+, the problem is retrieval. If it stays at 60%, the problem is generation (the LLM doesn't know how to use the retrieved context). 2) Oracle generation test: use a different LLM (e.g., GPT-4 instead of Llama 3) with the same retrieved context. If relevance improves, it's a generation weakness. If not, it's retrieval. 3) Slice analysis: by query type (simple fact, multi-hop, numerical) — retrieval may fail for multi-hop, generation may fail for numerical reasoning.

sections.title: Production RAG at 10M QPS
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: At 10M queries/day (~115 QPS avg, 400+ peak), every millisecond and every dollar counts. Three levers for production RAG: caching (avoid recomputation), pre-computation (move work offline), query optimization (make each query count). At this scale, the RAG system is no longer a simple retrieve-and-generate loop — it's a distributed system with multiple services, caching layers, and adaptive strategies.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Embedding cache: 80%+ hit rate for repeated queries (Zipf distribution — top 10% of queries account for 60-70% of traffic). With a 1M-entry LRU cache (1536d × 4 bytes = ~6GB), you serve 80% of retrieval queries from cache (0ms) instead of re-embedding (10-15ms). Query rewriting: HIPPO (query expansion with related terms) improves recall by 5-10%. HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings — generate a hypothetical answer, embed that instead of the query) improves retrieval by 5-15% for ambiguous queries. Multi-hop RAG: for complex questions, decompose into sub-questions, retrieve for each, aggregate — adds 100-500ms but improves accuracy by 10-25% on multi-step queries.

track_changes Production Architecture: 1) Query router: classify query as simple (single-hop, factoid) → fast path (one retrieve + generate) vs complex (multi-hop, comparative) → slow path (decompose, multi-retrieve, aggregate). 2) Embedding cache: LRU with TTL (invalidate on corpus update). 3) Pre-computed chunks: for static corpora (documentation, policy manuals), pre-compute embeddings once and serve from read-only replica. 4) Adaptive top-k: simple queries get top-3, complex queries get top-7 (more context needed). 5) Monitor and alert on: retrieval latency p95, cache hit rate, empty retrieval rate (0 relevant passages found — signals corpus gap), faithfulness score drift.

search Staff Engineer Question: "At 10M queries/day, your RAG system is costing $100K/month in LLM inference. The retrieval cache hit rate is 40%. Product says 10% improvement in answer quality is worth $20K/month extra. What do you do?" Answer: 1) Measure how much of the 60% cache miss rate is new queries vs corrupted cache (eviction). 2) If eviction-driven: increase cache size (6GB → 12GB, negligible cost). 3) Add semantic caching: for queries within cosine distance 0.05 of a cached query, return the cached answer directly — increases hit rate to 75%+ with <1% quality loss. 4) Deploy query rewriting (HIPPO) to improve recall for tail queries (the remaining 25% of misses). 5) For the 10% quality improvement, spend $20K on fine-tuning the generator with RAG-specific data (better faithfulness), not on more compute.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks (Lewis et al., 2020) — The original RAG paper. Introduces the RAG-Sequence and RAG-Token architectures. Foundational reading for any RAG system design interview.
  • RAGAS: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation (Es et al., 2023) — The RAGAS evaluation framework. Faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision, context recall. The standard for RAG evaluation in production.
  • HyDE: Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval without Relevance Labels (Gao et al., 2022) — Hypothetical Document Embeddings: generate a hypothetical answer, then use its embedding for retrieval. 5-15% improvement for ambiguous queries.
  • DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines (Khattab et al., 2024) — DSPy's RAG modules with automatic optimization of retrieval strategies, multi-hop decomposition, and prompt compilation.
  • SELF-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection (Asai et al., 2023) — RAG with learned reflection tokens: the model decides when to retrieve, whether passages are relevant, and whether the output is supported. Adds 5-10% quality at 2× cost.
  • When Not to Trust Your LLM: A Practical Guide to Hallucination Detection — Complements RAG with hallucination detection and grounding verification. Relevant for building reliable RAG systems.

details: RAG Decision Flow: Corpus size → chunk strategy (≤1M docs: fixed-256; >1M: semantic) → embedding model (≤1M queries/day: open-source BGE; >1M: managed OpenAI/Cohere) → index (≤10M vectors: Qdrant single-node; >10M: Milvus sharded) → retrieval (dense-only for speed; hybrid BM25+dense for recall) → reranking (skip if p95 < 200ms target; add cross-encoder if quality matters) → caching (embedding cache → semantic cache → full response cache) → monitor (HR@5, faithfulness, cache hit rate, latency p95).
